title: LIMS-RAG: Localized Minimal-Support Perturbations as a Practical Feature Variant for Sentence-Level RAG Verification
description: This project is a careful empirical study of one incremental detector feature for RAG faithfulness checking. Starting from a strong support-only sentence verifier, it tests whether localized perturbations around a greedily predicted support subset improve sentence-level verification beyond support alone, support-plus-compactness, and full-context removal.
motivation: Recent RAG work shows that support after the fact is weaker than faithful evidence use: a sentence can look supported somewhere in retrieved context without being truly evidence-dependent. Existing work already covers efficient support verification, claim-level diagnosis, coarse context sensitivity, synchronous faithfulness monitoring, and counterfactual attribution. The remaining practical question is narrower: whether localized support-set perturbation is a useful low-cost feature variant under realistic compute limits and under stricter controls for label ambiguity and search instability.
proposed_approach: For each answer sentence, retrieve and rerank candidate evidence from the benchmark-provided context, greedily construct a small support set `S_min`, and compute support, compactness, redundancy, and localized perturbation features by removing, swapping, or leave-one-out ablating only that subset. Train a simple logistic-regression detector and compare it primarily against support-only, support-plus-compactness, and full-context removal baselines, while also running threshold calibration and stability analyses for `S_min` and evaluating on strict sentence projections plus an uncertainty-aware audited subset.

related_work.title: MiniCheck: Efficient Fact-Checking of LLMs on Grounding Documents
related_work.authors: Liyan Tang, Philippe Laban, Greg Durrett
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides the support-verification baseline family; the proposed work adds a localized necessity feature on top of such a verifier rather than replacing it.

related_work.title: Auto-GDA: Automatic Domain Adaptation for Efficient Grounding Verification in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
related_work.authors: Tobias Leemann, Periklis Petridis, Giuseppe Vietri, Dionysis Manousakas, Aaron Roth, Sergul Aydore
related_work.how_it_differs: Improves efficient grounding verification through synthetic adaptation but remains support-centric rather than testing localized perturbation around a compact support subset.

related_work.title: RAGChecker: A Fine-grained Framework for Diagnosing Retrieval-Augmented Generation
related_work.authors: Dongyu Ru, Lin Qiu, Xiangkun Hu, Tianhang Zhang, Peng Shi, Shuaichen Chang, Cheng Jiayang, Cunxiang Wang, Shichao Sun, Huanyu Li, Zizhao Zhang, Binjie Wang, Jiarong Jiang, Tong He, Zhiguo Wang, Pengfei Liu, Yue Zhang, Zheng Zhang
related_work.how_it_differs: Already offers a broader claim-level diagnosis framework; this project is a much narrower in-setting test of one localized feature variant.

related_work.title: Synchronous Faithfulness Monitoring for Trustworthy Retrieval-Augmented Generation
related_work.authors: Di Wu, Jia-Chen Gu, Fan Yin, Nanyun Peng, Kai-Wei Chang
related_work.how_it_differs: Also performs sentence-level faithfulness monitoring, but during decoding with generation-dynamics signals; the proposed method is post-hoc and uses only benchmark context plus lightweight support scoring.

related_work.title: REFIND at SemEval-2025 Task 3: Retrieval-Augmented Factuality Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models
related_work.how_it_differs: Operationalizes coarse context sensitivity, whereas the proposed method focuses on localized perturbations around a predicted support subset.

related_work.title: Evidence Contextualization and Counterfactual Attribution for Conversational QA over Heterogeneous Data with RAG Systems
related_work.authors: Rishiraj Saha Roy, Joel Schlotthauer, Chris Hinze, Andreas Foltyn, Luzian Hahn, Fabian Kuech
related_work.how_it_differs: Uses counterfactual evidence removal for attribution; the proposed work adapts that intuition to a cheaper sentence-level detector feature.

related_work.title: Correctness is not Faithfulness in RAG Attributions
related_work.authors: Jonas Wallat, Maria Heuss, Maarten de Rijke, Avishek Anand
related_work.how_it_differs: Supplies the conceptual motivation that support can be post-rationalized; the proposal tests one practical detector signal for that gap.

related_work.title: SelfCheckGPT: Zero-Resource Black-Box Hallucination Detection for Generative Large Language Models
related_work.authors: Potsawee Manakul, Adian Liusie, Mark Gales
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides a sentence-level hallucination detection precedent based on self-consistency rather than retrieved-evidence perturbation.

related_work.title: RAGTruth: A Hallucination Corpus for Developing Trustworthy Retrieval-Augmented Language Models
related_work.authors: Cheng Niu, Yuanhao Wu, Juno Zhu, Siliang Xu, KaShun Shum, Randy Zhong, Juntong Song, Tong Zhang
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides the main benchmark and span annotations, but does not test localized support-set perturbations or explicit `S_min` stability analysis.

hypothesis: Localized perturbation features computed around a greedily predicted support set will yield small but robust gains over support-only, support-plus-compactness, and full-context removal on strict-label sentence verification in RAG, and those gains will remain positive across a locked neighborhood of `S_min` thresholds rather than depending on unstable search artifacts.
success_criteria: On the strict-label RAGTruth slice, the full detector improves over support-only by at least 1.0 macro-F1 point or 0.01 AUPRC.
The full detector also improves over support-plus-compactness and over full-context removal by at least 0.5 macro-F1 points or 0.005 AUPRC.
The performance gain remains directionally positive across adjacent locked `S_min` thresholds and does not disappear on the stable-`S_min` subset.

On the manually audited subset, localized perturbation improves discrimination between evidence-dependent and post-rationalized cases with bootstrap confidence intervals that do not make the effect indistinguishable from zero.
If gains appear only on permissive projected labels, only under unstable `S_min`, or only after flexible feature recombination, the hypothesis is treated as unsupported.
